proof: For m<=1, a witness Y has at most two subset images. For m=2, with c=f(emptyset), every pair {i,j} requires f({i}) and f({j}) to be distinct and both different from c; four singleton images would need to be pairwise distinct in the three-element set X\{c}, impossible. For m=3, the six pair values form the three perfect matchings of K4, so every 3-element Y contains one pair of each color 1,2,3, while f(emptyset)=0; hence every Y of size at least 3 maps onto X.
claim_boundary: Finite H(4)=3 only; no claim about the asymptotic Erdős 624 conjecture or H(5).
